content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

January 24, 1994

Julie Green
The Thayer Building
1228 N Street, Suite 9
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request For Informal
Assistance
Our File No. I-93-376

Dear Ms. Green:

This is in reply to your request for advice concerning the
campaign disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act.1/
Because your questions are general inquiries, we are treating your
letter as a request for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation
18329(c)(3).2/

QUESTIONS

1. If a committee gives money to a slate mailer organization
in order to support candidates that will be listed on the mailer,
would that be a payment to a slate mailer organization or a
contribution to the organization?

2. What is the name of the court case that would delete the
remaining provisions of Proposition 73 and reinstate parts of
Proposition 68?

3. What changes will be made to the 1994 campaign reporting
forms?

___________________________

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Julie Green
I-93-376
page 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. If the committee earmarks the payment for a specific
candidate be listed on the slate mailer, the committee would be
making a contribution to, or an independent expenditure to
support, that candidate and would not be making a contribution to
the slate mailer organization.

2. The court case is Walter B. Gerken, Melvin B. Lane, John
Larson, Cornell C. Maier, Roco C. Siciliano, Jean R. Wente,
Francisco M. Wheat and California Common Cause v. FPPC and
Franchise Tax Board, California Supreme Court, Case No. S025815.
On December 20, 1993, the Court, by a 4-3 vote, refused to put
Proposition 68 into effect.

3. For general purpose recipient committees, the Form 420
will be updated for 1994 to add descriptions of the expenditure
code committees use when itemizing expenditures and accrued
expenses on Schedules E and F.

ANALYSIS

Sections 82015 and 82025 define "contribution" and
"expenditure" in part as payments made for political purposes.
Regulations 18215 and 18225 provide that a payment is made for
"political purposes" if it is for the purpose of influencing the
action of the voters for or against the nomination or election of
a candidate or candidates, or the qualification or passage of any
measure or is received by, made at the behest of or made by:

(A) A candidate, unless it is clear from the surrounding
circumstances that the payment was made or received for
personal purpose unrelated to his or her candidacy or
status as an officeholder;

(B) A controlled committee; or

(C) An organization formed or existing primarily for
political purposes. (Regulations 18215(a) and
18225(a).)

"Made at the behest" of a candidate means that the payment
was made under the control or at the direction of, in cooperation,
consultation, coordination, or concert with or at the request or
suggestion of the candidate. (Regulation 18215(b).)

An "independent expenditure" is a payment in connection with
a communication which expressly advocates the election or defeat
of a clearly identified candidate or the passage or defeat of a
clearly identified ballot measure, but which is not made to or at
the behest of the affected candidate or committee. (Section
82031.)

Julie Green
93-376
page 3


     Generally, payments made to a slate mailer organization are
not contributions to the organization because it is presumed that
the organization is providing full and adequate consideration for
the payments received. (Finley Advice Letter, No. A-90-219.)
Instead, if a committee makes a payment to the slate mailer
organization at the behest of a candidate, the committee would be
making a non-monetary contribution to the candidate. If the
payment was made and earmarked to support a specific candidate but
was not made at the behest of the candidate, the committee would
be making an independent expenditure on behalf of the candidate.

     However, if the committee makes a payment to a slate mailer
organization to help defray the general costs of publishing a
slate mailer, the slate mailer organization would not be providing
full and adequate consideration for the payments received and the
committee's payment would be deemed a contribution to the slate
mailer organization. (Finley Advice Letter, supra.)

     If you have additional questions, please contact me at
(916) 322-5662.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Colleen McGee
                                        Political Reform Consultant
                                        Technical Assistance Division
